title: Understanding the Moment of Inertia
text: To calculate the torque, we first need to understand the moment of inertia for each blade. Since each blade is modeled as a uniform rod rotating about one end, the moment of inertia is given by the formula $$I=\frac{1}{3} m L^2$$. What is the moment of inertia for one blade?

title: Calculate the Moment of Inertia for One Blade
text: Using the formula $$I=\frac{1}{3} m L^2$$, where $$m$$ is the mass of the blade and L is the length of the blade, calculate the moment of inertia for one blade.

title: Total Moment of Inertia for the Rotor Assembly
text: Since the rotor assembly has three blades, the total moment of inertia is the sum of the moments of inertia of all three blades. How would you calculate this?

title: Calculate Total Moment of Inertia
text: Multiply the moment of inertia of one blade by the number of blades to find the total moment of inertia for the rotor assembly.

title: Understanding Angular Acceleration
text: To find the torque, we need to calculate the angular acceleration. The angular acceleration is the change in angular velocity over time. What is the change in angular velocity if the blades start from rest and reach a maximum rotation rate of $$20$$ $$\frac{rev}{min}$$?

title: Calculate Angular Acceleration
text: Convert the maximum rotation rate from revolutions per minute to radians per second, then divide by the time taken to reach this rate (5 minutes) to find the angular acceleration.

title: Calculate the Torque
text: Now that we have the total moment of inertia and the angular acceleration, we can calculate the torque using the formula $$torque=I \alpha$$, where I is the total moment of inertia and alpha is the angular acceleration.

title: Final Calculation of Torque
text: Using the formula $$torque=I \alpha$$, calculate the torque required to rotate the blades up to the maximum rotation rate.
